children.name: Alca
children.description: Razorbills — medium-sized auks of the North Atlantic, named for the distinctive laterally compressed bill that is crossed with a white line. The razorbill (tordmule) is the closest living relative of the extinct great auk and shares its stocky, black-and-white coloration and awkward gait on land. In Sweden, razorbills breed on rocky sea cliffs on the west coast, particularly on Stora Karlsö off Gotland and at Nidingen on the Kattegat. Like all auks, razorbills are consummate underwater fliers, using their wings to pursue fish at depth. They are highly colonial breeders, choosing crevices and ledges in cliff faces rather than the open ledges preferred by guillemots.

children.name: Alca torda
children.description: The tordmule — a stout, black-and-white auk of Swedish west coast sea cliffs. Its laterally compressed bill with white cross-line is unique among Swedish seabirds. Breeds colonially on Nidingen and other rocky islands off the Kattegat coast; winters in the North Sea and Atlantic. Lays a single egg in a rock crevice or cave. Like guillemots, razorbills can dive to 120 m in pursuit of fish. Closely related to, and possibly the living sister species of, the extinct great auk.

children.name: Pinguinus
children.description: The great auk genus — a monotypic genus now containing only the extinct Great Auk, the original 'penguin' of the North Atlantic. The great auk was the largest of the auks, flightless and superbly adapted for underwater pursuit of fish, breeding on remote rocky islands from Iceland to Newfoundland. Its extinction in 1844, driven by hunting for feathers, oil, and museum specimens, stands as one of the earliest and most documented examples of human-caused extinction. Southern-hemisphere penguins were named after the great auk by early mariners who encountered similarly rotund, flightless seabirds.

children.name: Pinguinus impennis
children.description: Extinct since 1844; the last confirmed pair was killed on Eldey Island, Iceland, on 3 June 1844 by collectors seeking museum specimens. Stood 75–85 cm tall with vestigial, flipper-like wings usable only underwater — one of the North Atlantic's supreme fish hunters. Bred on a handful of low, wave-washed rocky islands; its inability to fly made it defenceless against human hunters. One of the best-documented historical extinctions; fewer than 80 skins and 75 eggs survive in collections worldwide.

children.name: Uria
children.description: Guillemots — the most abundant large seabirds in the North Atlantic, nesting in enormous cliff colonies that can contain hundreds of thousands of birds. The common guillemot (sillgrissla) breeds in one of the densest aggregations of any bird: pairs share tiny cliff ledges so tightly that incubating birds are pressed shoulder to shoulder in ranks, exchanging constant mutual preening and loud guttural calls. Guillemots lay a single pear-shaped egg directly on the bare rock — the tapering shape means it rolls in a tight circle rather than off the ledge. Chicks jump from the colony to the sea before they can fly, at around three weeks old, and are tended at sea by the father. Sweden's guillemot colonies, concentrated on limestone stacks off Gotland and the Kattegat archipelago, are among the most important seabird sites in the Baltic region.

children.name: Uria aalge
children.description: The sillgrissla — Sweden's most colonial cliff-nesting seabird, forming massive breeding aggregations on Stora Karlsö off Gotland. Its upright posture and chocolate-brown upperparts distinguish it from the razorbill; 'bridled' forms with white eye-ring and spectacle line occur regularly. Dives to 180 m using wings to fly underwater. The colony at Stora Karlsö, with tens of thousands of birds, is one of the most spectacular wildlife sights in Sweden and is protected as a nature reserve.

children.name: Uria lomvia
children.description: The Arctic counterpart of the Common Murre, breeding in enormous cliff colonies from Svalbard to Alaska. Distinguished by its shorter, thicker bill with a pale stripe at the base. One of the deepest-diving auks, recorded to 210 m; specialises on Arctic cod in colder waters than the Common Murre. Known in Europe as Brünnich's Guillemot; an occasional winter visitor to Swedish waters.

children.name: Cepphus
children.description: Black guillemots — smaller, rounder auks that differ fundamentally from their relatives in breeding close inshore, in small dispersed groups rather than large colonies, and in remaining in their breeding areas year-round rather than migrating to open ocean. The black guillemot (tobisgrissla) is one of the most characteristic birds of the Swedish outer archipelago, breeding under boulders and in rock crevices on skerry islands throughout the Baltic and Kattegat coasts. In breeding plumage it is entirely jet black with a large white wing patch and vivid scarlet feet and gape — one of the most striking of all Swedish seabirds. Its piping calls are a defining sound of the Swedish summer archipelago. Unlike other auks it dives primarily for bottom-dwelling fish and invertebrates in relatively shallow coastal waters.

children.name: Cepphus grylle
children.description: The tobisgrissla — one of the most familiar and beloved birds of the Swedish summer archipelago. Its black plumage with white oval wing patch and brilliant scarlet legs and bill gape make it unmistakeable. Unlike other auks, it breeds in dispersed pairs rather than large colonies, nesting under boulders and in rock crevices on skerry islands. Year-round resident on the Swedish coast, turning largely white in winter. Dives for blennies, gobies, and sand eels in rocky nearshore habitats.

children.name: Cepphus columba
children.description: The Pacific counterpart of the Black Guillemot, distributed along Pacific coasts from Alaska and Kamchatka south to California and Japan. Distinguished from its Atlantic relative by dark wedge-markings within the white wing patch and darker underwings. Inhabits rocky coasts and kelp forests, foraging for small fish and invertebrates in the nearshore zone; breeds in small dispersed groups rather than large colonies. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Cepphus carbo
children.description: A distinctive Pacific auk breeding on cliff faces and boulder beaches around the Sea of Okhotsk, Sakhalin, and northern Japan. In breeding plumage it is uniformly dark brown-black with bold white eye-rings and streaks that create a 'spectacled' appearance. Winters at sea in the northern North Pacific; less gregarious than the Common Murre, favouring rocky coastal habitats. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Alle
children.description: Little auk — the smallest North Atlantic auk, barely larger than a starling yet one of the most abundant seabirds in the world, with breeding colonies in the High Arctic (particularly northwestern Greenland and Svalbard) numbering in the millions. The little auk (alkekung) is a highly pelagic bird, spending its non-breeding months far out in the North Atlantic and Barents Sea where it feeds on copepods and other tiny crustaceans. It very rarely approaches Swedish coasts voluntarily, but in years of severe autumn storms, exhausted and disoriented birds may be blown inland — 'wrecks' of little auks occasionally occur in Sweden when Atlantic gales sweep birds far from their usual pelagic range.

children.name: Alle alle
children.description: The alkekung — the world's most numerous auk, with hundreds of millions breeding on Arctic cliff screes from Svalbard to Greenland. Starling-sized and essentially pelagic outside the breeding season. An occasional visitor to Swedish waters, most often seen from the Kattegat coast in late autumn after Atlantic storms. In major 'wreck' years, exhausted birds may appear far inland at lakes and rivers. Its tiny bill is adapted for scooping up dense concentrations of planktonic copepods.

children.name: Fratercula
children.description: Puffins — the most iconic and instantly recognisable of all the auks, beloved for their clown-like colourful bill and upright posture. The Atlantic puffin (lunnefågel) breeds in burrows excavated in soft coastal turf on offshore islands. In Sweden, the main breeding sites are on Nidingen and Hallands Väderö off the Kattegat coast — the easternmost significant puffin colonies in Europe. Sweden's puffin population is small and has declined sharply since the mid-20th century, making each individual a precious sight. Puffins carry multiple sand eels crosswise in the bill simultaneously (using backward-facing serrations to hold earlier catches while catching more) — some individuals have been recorded carrying over 60 fish in a single bill-load.

children.name: Fratercula arctica
children.description: The lunnefågel — Sweden's most charismatic seabird, breeding in small numbers on Nidingen and Hallands Väderö off the Swedish west coast. Its extraordinary multicoloured bill — red, yellow, and blue-grey — is used in courtship displays and for catching fish; the colours fade in winter to a more subdued grey. Nests in burrows dug with bill and feet in soft clifftop soil. Sweden's population is tiny and declining, making it a priority species for Swedish seabird conservation. Spends eight months of the year far out in the Atlantic.

children.name: Fratercula corniculata
children.description: The Pacific counterpart of the Atlantic Puffin, breeding on cliffs and rocky islands from Alaska to Kamchatka. Named for the small, dark fleshy 'horn' (papilla) above each eye visible in breeding plumage. Like all puffins it carries multiple fish crosswise in its serrated bill; winters in the open North Pacific far from land. Its jet-black body, white face, and orange-red bill make it unmistakeable. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Fratercula cirrhata
children.description: The largest puffin, breeding along the Pacific Rim from California to the Aleutians and south to Japan. In breeding plumage it sports dramatic creamy-yellow head tufts drooping behind the eye, contrasting with a jet black body and brilliant orange-red bill. A powerful diver that pursues fish underwater to depth; nests in burrows on grassy clifftops and coastal slopes. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Brachyramphus
children.description: The murrelets — three cryptically plumaged Pacific auks that are unique in their nesting habits. Unlike most auks, which breed in cliff colonies or burrows, Brachyramphus murrelets nest inland, often deep in old-growth forests far from the sea. The Marbled Murrelet's nest was an ornithological mystery for most of the 20th century; when finally discovered in 1974 it revealed a single egg laid on a large mossy tree branch with no nest structure. This dependence on old-growth forest makes them highly sensitive to logging.

children.name: Brachyramphus marmoratus
children.description: A cryptically plumaged Pacific auk that commutes daily between coastal feeding waters and old-growth forest nesting sites, sometimes 80 km inland. Its nest was one of the last zoological mysteries of North American birds, undiscovered until 1974. Threatened by old-growth logging; listed as Threatened in the US, where it has become an iconic symbol of Pacific Northwest forest conservation. IUCN Endangered.

children.name: Brachyramphus perdix
children.description: An Asian counterpart of the Marbled Murrelet, breeding in forests of Siberia, Japan, and Korea — often over 100 km from the sea. Long treated as a subspecies of the Marbled Murrelet, confirmed as a distinct species in 1994 based on morphological and genetic differences. A vagrant to the western United States, where its appearance causes significant excitement among birders. IUCN Near Threatened.

children.name: Brachyramphus brevirostris
children.description: Named after German naturalist Friedrich Heinrich von Kittlitz who first collected specimens in Alaska. A small, pale-plumaged murrelet with an exceptionally close association with tidewater glaciers; a large proportion of its population breeds and forages near glacial ice in Alaska and Kamchatka. Dramatically declining as glaciers retreat due to climate change — one of the most climate-sensitive seabirds in the world. IUCN Near Threatened.

children.name: Synthliboramphus
children.description: Small Pacific murrelets, notable for the extraordinary precocity of their chicks: young leave the nest burrow within days of hatching, guided by parents calling from the sea, and complete all their development at sea. This strategy — unique among alcids — reduces the time chicks spend vulnerable to terrestrial predators. The genus includes the Ancient Murrelet of the North Pacific and Japan's only endemic auk, the Japanese Murrelet.

children.name: Synthliboramphus antiquus
children.description: A distinctive Pacific auk named for its grey plumage resembling an 'ancient' grey-haired appearance. One of the most unusual alcids: chicks leave their burrow within two to three days of hatching, at night, guided by calls from parents waiting at sea — and complete all development at sea. Breeds on offshore islands from British Columbia to Japan; a regular presence in offshore Pacific waters. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Synthliboramphus wumizusume
children.description: Japan's only endemic auk, breeding on rocky islands off southern Honshu, Kyushu, and the Izu Islands. A small, black-and-white murrelet with a dramatic dark crest in breeding plumage; departs for the open Pacific after breeding, making it rarely observed at sea. IUCN Vulnerable due to its restricted breeding range, introduced predators on island colonies, and fishery bycatch.

children.name: Synthliboramphus hypoleucus
children.description: A small Pacific alcid breeding mainly on Guadalupe Island and the San Benito Islands off Baja California. Formerly lumped with the Scripps's Murrelet as 'Xantus's Murrelet'; recognised as distinct based on plumage differences, vocal divergence, and genetic evidence. Threatened by introduced cats and rats on breeding islands; IUCN Endangered.

children.name: Synthliboramphus craveri
children.description: Named after Italian naturalist Federico Craveri, who studied Mexican flora and fauna in the 19th century. A small Pacific alcid endemic to the Gulf of California and adjacent Pacific coasts of Baja California. Breeds on rocky islands in the Gulf of California; winters in warmer offshore Pacific waters. IUCN Near Threatened due to introduced predators and fishing bycatch.

children.name: Synthliboramphus scrippsi
children.description: A small Pacific murrelet breeding on the California Channel Islands and northern Baja California offshore islands. Closely related to the Guadalupe Murrelet; the two were formally split from 'Xantus's Murrelet' based on plumage, genetic, and behavioural evidence. IUCN Near Threatened due to its restricted breeding range and ongoing nest predation by introduced rats and cats on island colonies.

children.name: Ptychoramphus
children.description: A monotypic genus containing Cassin's Auklet — one of the most abundant seabirds in the California Current ecosystem. Unlike the ornately plumaged Aethia auklets, Cassin's is cryptically dark and essentially featureless; it relies on darkness for safety at colonies, landing only at night. Its importance in marine food webs as a euphausiid consumer is disproportionate to its unassuming appearance.

children.name: Ptychoramphus aleuticus
children.description: A small, sooty-grey auklet of Pacific North American waters from the Aleutian Islands to Baja California. One of the most abundant seabirds in the California Current; feeds almost entirely on euphausiid shrimp and copepods scooped in its expandable gular pouch. Breeds in burrows on offshore islands; exclusively nocturnal at colonies to avoid predation. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Aethia
children.description: The crested and ornamental auklets of the Bering Sea — small, highly social Pacific auks that form breeding colonies numbering in the millions on Aleutian and Russian island rock fields. Several species emit a striking citrus-like odour from glands at the base of the bill that is thought to repel ectoparasites and may play a role in mate selection — an extraordinary chemical communication system unique among seabirds. Spectacular synchronised display flights around colonies, known as 'roller-coaster' flights, are among the most impressive seabird behaviours in the world.

children.name: Aethia psittacula
children.description: A chunky Bering Sea auklet with a bizarre, rounded orange-red bill resembling a parrot's (psittacula = 'little parrot'). Unlike other Aethia auklets it feeds on jellyfish and large zooplankton rather than small crustaceans, and the peculiar bill shape is likely an adaptation for grasping slippery prey. Breeds on rocky Aleutian and Russian islands; performs synchronised aerial courtship displays. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Aethia pusilla
children.description: The smallest auk in the world at just 16 cm, yet one of the most numerous seabirds on Earth; the colonies on St. Lawrence Island, Alaska, contain tens of millions of birds. Feeds on copepods and other tiny crustaceans, concentrating them in a specialised sublingual pouch. Breeds in boulder fields; the noise and musky scent of large colonies are overwhelming at close range. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Aethia pygmaea
children.description: A small, sociable auklet of the Aleutian Islands and Kuril Islands, bearing three white facial plumes and a forward-curling dark crest. Groups roost communally on rocky islets in fast tidal rips. Unusually, individuals are attracted to the citrus-like scent produced by conspecifics — a behaviour that may facilitate kin recognition and reduce parasite loads on communal roost sites. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Aethia cristatella
children.description: The largest Aethia auklet, forming breeding colonies of hundreds of thousands to millions on Aleutian and Kuril island boulder fields. Both sexes carry a prominent forward-curling black crest and pale iris; the colony emits a powerful tangerine-like scent from auklets' uropygial secretions, which appear to reduce tick burdens. Feeds almost entirely on euphausid shrimp in cold Bering Sea and North Pacific waters. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Cerorhinca
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Rhinoceros Auklet — a large, puffin-related alcid of Pacific North American and Asian coasts. Despite its auklet name it is phylogenetically closer to puffins than to the small Aethia auklets. Its most striking feature is a horn-like projection at the base of the upper mandible that develops only in the breeding season; combined with white facial plumes, it gives the bird a bizarrely ornate appearance.

children.name: Cerorhinca monocerata
children.description: A large, brown alcid of Pacific North American and Asian coasts, named for the curious horn-like projection at the base of its bill during the breeding season. Related to puffins despite its superficially different appearance; nests in deep burrows on forested offshore islands. Exclusively nocturnal at colonies to avoid gull predation; feeds primarily on small fish, especially herring and sand lance, carried crosswise in the bill to chicks. IUCN Least Concern.
